content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 12, 1993

Robert E. Leidigh
Olson, Hagel, Fong, Leidigh, Waters & Fishburn
300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-93-130a

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

     This is in reply to your April 27, 1993, request for advice
concerning the duties of Hawthorne City Councilmember David York
and Charles Bookhammer, and Mayor George Steven Andersen, under
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act")¹.

     As you are aware, we previously issued a letter to you dated
April 12, 1993, on this matter.  (See Leidigh Advice Letter, No.
I-93-130.)  In that letter we declined to issue formal advice
because you requested a formal reply within six working days on a
matter which was in litigation and on which no formal order had
issued.  We felt, under the circumstances, that there was
insufficient information to support formal advice at that point.

     In your letter of April 27, you state that you believe the
previous information you provided was sufficient for us to render
a formal decision.  You further state that you will invoke the
"self-implementing immunizing" provisions of Government Code
Section 83114(b) once the 21-working day period has expired if no
formal written advice is forthcoming.  You calculated the "self
immunization" period to begin on May 3, 1993.

     Also, in the additional materials you provided with your
April 27 letter is a letter to you from Glenn R. Watson whose law
firm represents the City of Hawthorne in the litigation relating
to proposed actions of your clients.  In his letter, Mr. Watson
characterizes our April 12 letter as expressing "apparent,

___________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

File No. A-93-130a
Page 2


although perhaps unwitting, partisanship," presumably against your
clients and the city of Hawthorne.

     We do not wish to belabor the issue of whether we were
provided sufficient facts in your original request in order to
issue a formal decision.  Suffice it to say that the litigation
status of this matter, combined with the information presented and
the request for our expedited action, caused us sufficient concern
to proceed cautiously.  (See Government Code Section 18329,
subdivisions (b)(1)(B) and (b)(8)(C).)

     Furthermore, we find objectionable Mr. Watson's suggestion
that the Commission acted in a partisan manner by previously
declining to issue formal advice.  If anything, this statement
illustrates the partisan and litigious atmosphere which apparently
surrounds the city's proposed actions and underscores why we felt
the need to proceed cautiously in giving advice.

     However, since you have now provided extensive additional
information on this matter and its current status, we are prepared
to render formal advice.
                                    QUESTIONS

     1.  If Councilmember York, Councilmember Bookhammer and Mayor
Andersen are disqualified from participating in a decision by the
five-member Hawthorne City Council to acquire property for open
space use by process of condemnation, may the rule of "legally
required participation" be invoked to "bring back" a sufficient
number of councilmembers to make the following decisions?

          (a)  Determination of just compensation to be paid for the
condemnee's property, environmental approval, and related actions
requiring expenditures of city funds such as for an appraiser,
counsel, and other experts, all of which require a quorum of three
councilmembers in order to take these actions.

          (b)  Adoption of a resolution of necessity, which is required
for condemnation of property by a public agency possessing those
powers.  A minimum of four affirmative votes are required in order
to take such action.

     2.  If the rule of "legally required participation" is invoked,
will the random selection of one of the disqualified
councilmembers for the decisions in Question 1(a) suffice so that
only one additional councilmember must be randomly selected for
the decision in Question 1(b), or is a new random selection of two
councilmembers required for the decision in Question 1(b)?

                                 CONCLUSIONS

     1.  The rule of "legally required participation" may be invoked
to requalify a sufficient number of councilmembers to make the
government decisions set forth in Questions 1(a) and 1(b) above.

File No. A-93-130a
Page 3


2. If the rule of "legally required participation" is invoked,
the random selection of one of the disqualified councilmembers to
make the government decisions set forth in Question 1(a) above
will suffice so that only one additional councilmember must be
randomly selected for the government decision set forth in
Question 1(b).

FACTS

The City of Hawthorne has initiated a condemnation proceeding
concerning a strip of land adjacent to the San Diego Freeway,
which traverses the city. It has been determined that three of
the five members of the city council, specifically, Councilmembers
York and Bookhammer, and Mayor Andersen, have a conflict of
interest with regards to city decisions relating to this
proceeding and normally would be disqualified under the Act from
participating in these decisions.

By law, a four vote supermajority is necessary for the city
to adopt the resolution of necessity which is required before the
city may commence this action. Other city proceedings concerning
the condemnation action require a three vote majority of the city
council.

On December 24, 1992, in the case of City of Hawthorne v.
Paul B. Del Valle, et al. (Los Angeles County Superior Court, Case
No. BC 046104) the court dismissed the city's eminent domain
proceeding in this matter and prohibited the city from applying
the so called "rule of necessity" by which some of the
disqualified city councilmembers could be permitted to vote. In
an order dated April 14, 1993 the court revised its December 24,
1992 order and permitted the city to act in this matter on the
condition that it complies "with all applicable laws." You
indicate that this permits the city, if necessary and appropriate,
to apply the Act's rule of "legally required participation."

ANALYSIS

General Discussion of "Legally Required Participation" Exception

Section 87101 permits an official who is otherwise
disqualified from participation under Section 87100 to make or
participate in the making of a governmental decision when the
official's participation is legally required.

Regulation 18701 provides that this exception does not apply
when there is an alternative source of decision consistent with
the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing the decision
(Regulation 18701(a)); when the disqualified official's vote is
merely needed to break a tie (Regulation 18701(c)(1)); or where a
quorum can be convened of other members of the agency who are not
disqualified under Section 87100, whether or not such other
disqualified under Section 87100, whether or not such other
members are actually present at the time of the disqualification
(Regulation 18701(c)(2)).

File No. A-93-130a
Page 4


You have provided no facts indicating that there is an
alternative source through which the city can make these
decisions. On this basis, we presume that there is none.2
Further, you have not indicated that subdivisions (c)(1) or (c)(2)
of Regulation 18701 are at issue. Therefore, so long as the city
applies the appropriate procedure in selecting the disqualified
officials who can participate in these decisions and those
officials follow the procedures set forth in subdivision (b) of
Regulation 18701, the rule of legally required participation will
apply. (A copy of Regulation 18701(b) is enclosed for your
convenience.)

Selection Process

To apply the "legally required participation" exception, not
all officials who have been disqualified can be requalified to
vote, but only the minimum number sufficient to permit the public body to
act. (In re Hudson (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 13, 17.) To select who
those officials are, the preferred method of selection is by any
random means which is impartial and equitable." (In re Hudson
(1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 13, 18.)

Where, as here, more than one decision will be necessary on
the issue that creates a conflict of interest, the question is
whether the random selection must be made for each decision. We
have previously advised that the random selection procedure need
not be repeated with respect to a series of decisions involving
the same general subject matter and the same disqualifying
interests. (O'Toole Advice Letter, No. I-92-630; Hopkins Advice
Letter, No. A-82-088.)

In the case of the City of Hawthorne, the city council must
make a series of decisions concerning condemnation of the subject
property. All of the decisions except one, the resolution of
necessity, require a three-vote majority. The resolution of
necessity requires a four-vote supermajority. In the three-vote
decisions, only one of the three disqualified city councilmembers
must be requalified. In the four-vote decision, two of the three
disqualified city councilmembers must be requalified.

Applying our previous advice to these facts, the random
selection of one of the disqualified city councilmembers to
participate in the three-vote decisions (listed in Question 1(a)
above) will permit that councilmember to participate in all of the
subsequent decisions, including the resolution of necessity
decision, in the condemnation proceeding. When it becomes
necessary to make the resolution of necessity decision, a random
selection from the two remaining disqualified councilmembers may

2 As you point out in your letter, the Commission, in its advice-
giving role, is not a fact finder. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC
Ops. 71.)

File No. A-93-130a
Page 5


be made and that councilmember may only participate in that
particular decision.

     Should you have further questions, please contact me at your
convenience.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Scott Halabrin
                                        Assistant General Counsel

SH:ak

Enclosure

In that letter we declined to issue formal advice because you requested a formal reply within six working days on a matter which was in litigation and on which no formal order had issued. We felt, under the circumstances, that there was insufficient information to support formal advice at that point. In your letter of April 27, you state that you believe the previous information you provided was sufficient for us to render a formal decision. You further state that you will invoke the "self-implementing immunizing" provisions of Government Code Section 83114(b) once the 21-working day period has expired if no formal written advice is forthcoming.
